---
title: Building a full package website with Quarto
---

## Overview

For the vast majority of R packages,
[pkgdown](https://pkgdown.r-lib.org/index.html) is the right tool. It is
mature, well-supported, and requires almost no configuration to produce a
polished package site.

If you have a specific reason to use a Quarto site — perhaps you want Quarto's
`freeze` capability to capture examples that depend on local resources (a
database, Spark, an LLM), or you want to unify R and Python documentation in a
single site — then `pkgsite` gives you a practical path forward. The
`pkgsite` repository itself is a working example of this approach, and the
sections below walk through each of its moving parts.

## The minimum viable pieces

A `pkgsite`-powered Quarto website needs four things:

1. A `_quarto.yml` file that describes the site and wires up `pkgsite`.
2. An `index.qmd` that wraps your `README.md`.
3. A `changelog.qmd` that wraps your `NEWS.md`.
4. A `_post-render.R` script that runs `downlit` so every function name in
   the rendered HTML becomes a hyperlink.

The reference pages are generated separately by `write_reference()` and then
included in the site through the navbar.

## `_quarto.yml`

The `_quarto.yml` file in the `pkgsite` repository is a good starting point.
The key sections are:

```yaml
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
  post-render: _post-render.R

execute:
  freeze: true
  eval: true

website:
  title: mypkg
  site-url: https://myorg.github.io/mypkg
  navbar:
    left:
      - sidebar:articles
      - href: reference/index.qmd
        text: Reference
      - href: changelog.qmd
        text: News
    right:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/myorg/mypkg

pkgsite:
  dir: "."
  reference:
    dir: reference
    index:
      file: index.qmd
```

A few things worth noting:

- `output-dir: docs` matches the default GitHub Pages convention of serving
  from the `docs/` folder of the `main` branch.
- `post-render: _post-render.R` hooks in the `downlit` pass after every
  render (see below).
- `freeze: true` is what makes `pkgsite` especially useful for examples that
  depend on local infrastructure. Render once locally, commit `_freeze/`, and
  GitHub only needs to assemble HTML — not re-execute any R code.
- The `pkgsite:` block tells the package where to find your source and where
  to write the reference pages.

## `index.qmd` and `changelog.qmd` — wrapping Markdown files

The `.qmd` wrappers for `README.md` and `NEWS.md` are intentionally thin. Each
one uses Quarto's `include` shortcode to embed the source Markdown verbatim at
render time:

```markdown
---
toc: true
---

{{</* include README.md */>}}
```

```markdown
---
toc: true
---

{{</* include NEWS.md */>}}
```

The key benefit is avoiding duplicate content. `README.md` and `NEWS.md` remain
the single source of truth — you edit them, and the website picks up the changes
automatically on the next render. The `.qmd` wrappers themselves stay static;
you only touch them when making a layout change (adding a front-matter option,
inserting a custom section), never for content changes.

Because `NEWS.md` uses standard Markdown headings for each version, Quarto
automatically builds a table of contents from them, which makes it easy for
users to jump to a specific release.

One naming note: the changelog wrapper is called `changelog.qmd` rather than
`news.qmd`. If the wrapper were named `news.qmd`, Quarto and R's documentation
tooling would both try to write an `news.html` output file, causing a collision
where one process deletes the file before the other can finish with it. Using a
different name sidesteps the conflict entirely.

## `_post-render.R` — adding function links with `downlit`

After Quarto builds the HTML files, [`downlit`](https://downlit.r-lib.org/)
scans each one and turns bare function names into hyperlinks pointing at their
documentation pages. The script is registered in `_quarto.yml` via the
`post-render` key (already shown in the example above) and runs automatically
on every render.

The full script, setup instructions, and the corresponding GitHub Actions
workflow are covered in the [GitHub Pages article](github-actions.qmd).

## Generating the reference pages

Call `write_reference()` once to create (or update) all the `.qmd` files under
`reference/`:

```r
pkgsite::write_reference()
```

This reads your package's `NAMESPACE` and roxygen2 documentation and writes one
`.qmd` per exported topic, plus a `reference/index.qmd` that lists them all. You
only need to re-run this when you add or remove exported functions; the existing
files stay frozen in `_freeze/` otherwise.

::: {.callout-important}
## When to clear the freeze cache

Quarto only re-executes a `.qmd` file when its source text changes. This means
two common situations will produce stale output even after a normal `quarto render`:

- **A function's example output changed** — for example, you fixed a bug and
  the printed result is now different, but the `.qmd` source is identical.
- **You changed a `_quarto.yml` setting** that affects execution, such as
  `eval` or `freeze`.

In both cases the fix is to delete the frozen output for the reference pages
and re-render locally:

```bash
rm -rf _freeze/reference
quarto render
```

If `pkgsite:` is configured with a custom `reference.dir`, replace `reference`
with that path. After re-rendering, commit the updated `_freeze/` folder so
GitHub picks up the new output.
:::

## Putting it all together

With the files above in place, a full local render is:

```bash
quarto render
```

Quarto executes any unfrozen `.qmd` files, writes HTML to `docs/`, and then
calls `_post-render.R` to add `downlit` links. For how to commit the results
and automate publishing, see the [GitHub Pages article](github-actions.qmd).
